message.content: Adversaries may exfiltrate data using a symmetrically encrypted network protocol distinct from the command and control (C2) channel, potentially sending data to an alternate network location. Symmetric encryption involves shared keys for both encryption and decryption, requiring pre-arranged key exchange. While network protocols often use symmetric encryption post key-exchange, adversaries might manually share keys and employ symmetric algorithms like RC4 or AES. This can lead to multiple encryption layers in already encrypted protocols (e.g., HTTPS) or introduce encryption to typically unencrypted protocols (e.g., HTTP, FTP). The impact includes unauthorized data transfer and potential data breaches. Common exploitation methods involve leveraging less monitored or non-standard protocols to evade detection. Mitigation strategies include network segmentation, monitoring for unusual data flows, implementing strict data exfiltration policies, and using advanced network intrusion detection systems to identify and block suspicious encrypted traffic.
